title: I tried to run Ampush while simultaneously spinning up new ventures, and none of them got real attention
cost: Two companies launched without a dedicated CEO effectively shut down. Ampush X, the internal new-ventures arm, went nowhere because Jesse was perpetually pulled back to client calls and never engaged at the level the new work required.
change: I committed to never starting a company without a CEO co-founder in place first, and I stopped trying to run the day-to-day of an existing business while launching new ones simultaneously.

receipts.quote: The number one thing we've learned through the journey is the company doesn't start until there's the CEO and co-founder in place and we only learned that the hard way — there was two companies we started without a CEO for either of them and both of them ended up kind of going through their own really really challenging... both of them effectively have shut down.

Ampush X went nowhere because it was like a weekly meeting with me and two employees who were not staffed on any clients so that like, Oh, let's go, let's go start a new business idea. And it just went nowhere because I wasn't engaged at the level I needed to be. Cause I would like, every time a client called, I would, I would run away.
receipts.source: What Happened to Our Partnership? — Mixergy (Andrew Warner × Jesse Pujji)

receipts.quote: The second I made that choice I should have basically stepped away as the CEO of the day-to-day ampush and put a GM in charge instead. I tried to run ampush while all these things happened and none of them got the time of day because of that.

title: I took Ampush through a full sale process and came back with numbers far below what we expected
cost: The team was targeting a 9-figure exit and received only 8-figure offers — a gap of tens of millions of dollars. They ultimately did not sell on those terms and had to restructure, bringing in a minority partner instead.
change: I partnered with Red Ventures as a minority investor, used their expertise to convert a fast-growing but not super-profitable company into one that actually made money, and internalized that growth without profitability destroys exit multiples.

receipts.quote: We took the business out for a sale process, but ended up with numbers below our expectations. (We were hoping for 9 figures and got 8 figures.) Crestfallen, we decided not to sell but partnered with and sold a minority stake to Red Ventures... They took our fast-growing but not super-profitable company and taught us how to make money.
receipts.source: How I Built These Companies - Bootstrapped Giants

receipts.quote: When they invested in ampush I cut the head count under their sort of tutelage by more than half and our revenue grew during that time. They're very good at like truly challenging the bloat in an organization — how many people do we actually need.

title: I launched a DTC brand (a digestive health product) without genuine founder-market fit and the economics collapsed immediately
cost: Spent ten thousand dollars on ads and generated only ten leads at a thousand dollars cost per lead against a fifty-dollar revenue per lead — a total unit-economics failure that nearly ended the experiment before it started.
change: I recognized I was not the customer, did not understand their problems deeply enough, and needed to only enter markets where I had real unfair advantage — knowing the customer, their language, and their purchase behavior from the inside.

receipts.quote: We spend ten grand and remember, we're getting fifty dollars per lead and we get ten leads. So fifty dollar revenue per lead and thousand dollar cost per lead and the floor just dropped out from underneath us. I mean we were shit scared.
receipts.source: A Primer on Performance Marketing - Happy Scribe

receipts.quote: Someone was like dude but jesse you know that customer you know their problems you had solved this problem in a prior life... you don't know digestive issues like you're not they're not that customer you don't know their problem like sure you can buy facebook ads but... it was a really good lesson in founder market fit.

I've always done B2B. And my only B2C orientation has been doing growth marketing for brands who already knew who their customers were… 'Well, who's our customer, Jesse? Do we have a perspective on that?' And I was like, 'Oh, like everyone poops, like, I don't know, tests, like try stuff.'
receipts.source: How DTC Brands Boom and Bust - Mission.org

title: I spent years operating inside my zone of excellence rather than my zone of genius, which left me burnt out and operating at roughly 30% of my potential impact
cost: By his own coach's assessment Jesse was spending only about 30% of his time in his zone of genius — meaning the majority of his working hours were draining rather than energizing him, and his effectiveness as a leader was correspondingly diminished. He also spent six months after transitioning out of Ampush not going to work until 11am and leaving at 4pm, questioning everything.
change: I committed to designing my role at Gateway X entirely around my zones of genius — coaching, starting businesses, building relationships — and stopped doing day-to-day operational delivery, explicitly refusing advisory roles in businesses I hadn't invested in so I would only engage where I was truly committed.

receipts.quote: you're at 75, 80 percent of your time is in your genius... you're clearly not there. You're a 30%. So not only are you less effective, but you're also more burnt out.
receipts.source: What Happened to Our Partnership? — Mixergy (Andrew Warner × Jesse Pujji)

receipts.quote: I like learned my zone of genius at Ampush. But I wasn't actually willing to commit to my zone of genius until I started GatewayX.
receipts.source: What Happened to Our Partnership? — Mixergy (Andrew Warner × Jesse Pujji)

receipts.quote: For 6 months, I didn't go into work until 11 am and left at 4 pm. I had no idea what was next and questioned everything... I started working with Dave Kashen, and it has been life-changing.
receipts.source: How I Built These Companies - Bootstrapped Giants
